---
title: "Lab Notebook"
format: html
toc: true
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Date

## Goals

## Insights

## 

# Feb 05, 2026

Looking at the 16s and sylph data it seems like the general major taxa
are all similar however some of the minor taxa are different.
**Question:** How sensitive are each method of taxonomy, which do we
trust? **Next Steps:**

-   Split phyla into autotrophs and heterotrophs and mixotrophs

-   Incorporate env metadata (O2 measurements as measure of respiration
    and carbon consumed?)

-   Research into how I should go about sorting through this data

-   Research into how the 16S data vs the metagenomic data works

-   Keep working on getting binning data

-   *Figure out if I need to normalize the data and how?*

```{r}
source("scripts/01_intro_script.R")
```

# Feb 06, 2026

Started looking at top 20 families. Code is in the bottom of
"sylph_mircoeco". Also created spreadsheet with names of families and
comments about what I could find out about their metabolism or ecology.

-   Continue this work -

-   Email Jay to get temp data

# Feb 10, 2026

**Questions:** Chitinophagaceae diversity at WM03. Is there a way to
look into how confident we are about that? What is the metagenome data
that I am seeing?

I am seeing a lot of variety within some of these families. I wonder if
I should start to filter out some of the smaller taxa?

Setup a script to isolate families - at the end of sylph_microeco
**Chitinophagaceae** - mostly just one genus and species though within
the most common genus (sedimini..) there was a ton of diversity at WM03.
Maybe points to errors in sequencing?

**Nitrosomonadaceae** - Lots of ammonia oxidizing to nitrate,
nitrosomonas more abundant in the spring but nitrosospira abundant in
the winter time. Is there functional redundancy here?

**Sphingomonadaceae** - More diversity in species within this family.
There is not a ton of information exept some that might suggest they are
aerobic and some strains were found in freshwater sediments and drinking
water

Plotted abundances and saw a huge drop in abundance from WM16 to WM22
(April to June)

For next time:
https://library.virginia.edu/data/articles/starting-non-metric-multidimensional-scaling-nmds

# Feb 16, 2026

-   Imported matlab data to R. Used claude to get intial scripts to
    import the .mat file into python and then write to csvs. At the
    bottom of "Intro Scripts" there is a chunk to write all of those to
    a dataframe in R. Matlab date is since jan 1, 1900 rather than jan
    1, 1970.
-   Decided to rewrite the matlab import in R, kept at the bottom of
    01_intro_scripts. Updated `sw_meta$strat_season` based on first 40m
    of temperature. Summer = stratification, fall = post summer
    isothermal period, winter = reverse stratification, spring = post
    winter isothermal Found some cool plots that look different based on
    the temperature but both seem to show seasonal differences.

```{r}
source("scripts/01_intro_script.R") # if needed
tb2 <- trans_beta$new(mt_sylph, group = "strat_season", measure = "bray")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "NMDS")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "NMDS")
tb2$cal_manova(manova_all = TRUE)
print(tb2$res_manova)

tb2$plot_ordination(plot_color = "strat_season", plot_shape = "strat_season", plot_type = c("point", "ellipse")) +
  geom_arrow_chain(colour = "black") +
    ggtitle("Sylph NMDS")

  tb2 <- trans_beta$new(meco_16s, group = "strat_season", measure = "bray")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "NMDS")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "NMDS")
tb2$cal_manova(manova_all = TRUE)
print(tb2$res_manova)

tb2$plot_ordination(plot_color = "strat_season", plot_shape = "strat_season", plot_type = c("point", "ellipse")) +
  geom_arrow_chain(colour = "black") +
    ggtitle("16s NMDS") +
  NULL
```

# Feb 17, 2026

Added the 24 hour temperature centered on the sampling day. Ended up
making the 14 day average. **TODO:** create a couple of figures to show
cody the differences in 16s vs sylph data. Plot temperature on these
axis, maybe look at PCoA? - Also trim the temperature data to have the
same number of days averaged before the first station.

\# Feb 18, 2026 Created a couple of abundance plots to show differences
between 16s data and sylph data. Moved a bunch of plots scirpts to the
02_plots script. Based on differences in how families change continue to
keep writing down my findings. **Created a script to look at graphs of
the most abundant families.**

**TODO** - How to deal with the difference between the mag data and the
16s data?

-   Show NDMS graphs

-   Show temperature determination

-   Binning script?

-   missing merged.fasta file

-   Committee - Jay - Ted - Bill - Formal Thesis Proposal? - Job Stuff.

**First work on binning stuff, then lefse, then think about committee
things.**

```{r}
  # Set rank (k, p, c, etc) and number of taxa to include
rank <- "o"
n <- 20

# Create abundance object
ts1 <- trans_abund$new(mt_sylph, taxrank = rank, ntaxa = n)

# Plot abunances
abund_s <- ts1$plot_bar(others_color = "grey70", xtext_keep = TRUE, legend_text_italic = FALSE, facet = "strat_season") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size=9)) +
  ggtitle("Sylph Data", subtitle = paste(n, "most abundant", rank))
abund_s

plotly_build(abund_s)

# Do the same but for 16s data
ts1 <- trans_abund$new(meco_16s, taxrank = rank, ntaxa = n)

# Plot abunances
abund_16 <- ts1$plot_bar(others_color = "grey70", xtext_keep = TRUE, legend_text_italic = FALSE, facet = "strat_season") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1, size=9)) +
  ggtitle("16s Data", subtitle = paste(n, "most abundant", rank))
abund_16
```

# Plot just a single taxa abundance

```{r}
# Clone dataset
taxa <- "Chitinophagaceae" # taxa of interest
n <- 20 # Number of taxa to included in final graph
mt_sylph_subset <- clone(mt_sylph)

###mt_sylph_subset include the taxa below
mt_sylph_subset$tax_table <- mt_sylph_subset$tax_table |> 
  filter(f == taxa)
mt_sylph_subset$tidy_dataset()

# check
mt_sylph_subset # OK
head(mt_sylph_subset$tax_table)

# recalculate abundances (needed?)
mt_sylph_subset$cal_abund()
mt_sylph_subset$taxa_abund

# plot:

t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "g", ntaxa = n)
t1$plot_bar(facet = "strat_season") +
  ggtitle(taxa)

# Clone dataset
taxa <- "Nitrosomonadaceae" # taxa of interest
n <- 20 # Number of taxa to included in final graph
mt_sylph_subset <- clone(mt_sylph)

###mt_sylph_subset include the taxa below
mt_sylph_subset$tax_table <- mt_sylph_subset$tax_table |> 
  filter(f == taxa)
mt_sylph_subset$tidy_dataset()

# check
mt_sylph_subset # OK
head(mt_sylph_subset$tax_table)

# recalculate abundances (needed?)
mt_sylph_subset$cal_abund()
mt_sylph_subset$taxa_abund

# plot:

t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "g", ntaxa = n)
t1$plot_bar(facet = "strat_season") +
  ggtitle(taxa)


# Clone dataset
taxa <- "Sphingomonadaceae" # taxa of interest
n <- 20 # Number of taxa to included in final graph
mt_sylph_subset <- clone(mt_sylph)

###mt_sylph_subset include the taxa below
mt_sylph_subset$tax_table <- mt_sylph_subset$tax_table |> 
  filter(f == taxa)
mt_sylph_subset$tidy_dataset()

# check
mt_sylph_subset # OK
head(mt_sylph_subset$tax_table)

# recalculate abundances (needed?)
mt_sylph_subset$cal_abund()
mt_sylph_subset$taxa_abund

# plot:

t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "g", ntaxa = n)
t1$plot_bar(facet = "strat_season") +
  ggtitle(taxa)


# Nested plot
# t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "s", ntaxa = n, show = 0, high_level = "g", high_level_fix_nsub = 10)
# t1$plot_bar(ggnested = T) |> plotly::ggplotly()
```

# Temp Plots

```{r}
#import temp file
mat_df <- readMat.default(con = "data/metadata/LSEO24h_temp.mat")

# Extract dataframe we want from 'T' for temp and convert matlab time to poxit time
temp_df <- mat_df$T |> 
  as.data.frame()
# Set colnames and rownames
colnames(temp_df) <- mat_df$dep  
rownames(temp_df) <- mat_df$t 
# move dttm column out, convert to datetime and pivot long
temp_df_long <- temp_df |> 
  rownames_to_column(var = "dttm") |>
  mutate(dttm = as_datetime((as.numeric(dttm) - 719529) * 86400)) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = !dttm, names_to = "depth", values_to = "temp") |> 
# Add averaged temperature column
  mutate(date = round_date(dttm, unit = "day"), 
  depth = as.numeric(depth)) |> 
  group_by(date, depth) |> 
  mutate(temp_c_24h_avg = mean(temp)) |> 
  ungroup()

## Create a 14 day averaged temperature---------------------------------
locs <- seaprocess::find_near(vec = as_date(temp_df_long$date), vals = as_date(sw_meta$date))
dates <- as_date(rep(NA, nrow(temp_df_long)))
for(i in length(locs):1) {
dates[1:locs[i]] <- as_date(sw_meta$date[i])
}
rm(i)
# Add the 14ish day average
temp_df_long<-temp_df_long |>
  mutate(sample_date = dates) |> 
  group_by(sample_date, depth) |> 
  mutate(temp_c_14d_avg = mean(temp)) |> 
  ungroup()

temp_plot <- temp_df_long |> 
  mutate(depth = as.numeric(depth), color = depth) |> 
  filter(depth < 50) |> 
  ggplot(aes(dttm, temp_c_24h_avg)) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = sw_meta$date) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = sw_meta$date[which(sw_meta$sample == sw_meta$sample[6:10])], color = "red") +
    geom_point() +
    scale_color_grey() +
    geom_point(data = temp_df_long[temp_df_long$depth == 38,], color = "red") +
    theme(legend.position = "none") + 
    theme_classic()

temp_plot


```

# Diversity Plot

```{r}
mt_sylph$alpha_diversity |>
  rownames_to_column("sample") |> 
  ggplot(aes(sample, Simpson, group = 1)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() +
  ggtitle("Sylph")


meco_16s$alpha_diversity |>
  rownames_to_column("sample") |> 
  ggplot(aes(sample, Simpson, group = 1)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_line() + 
  ggtitle("16s")

```

# Interpretation

See a big drop in diversity at WM19 in both 16s and sylph, both are less
consistent in the initial months. WM19 is at the end of April beginning
of May when the water column is weakly stratified.

# Feb 19, 2026

Looking at MAG families:

## **Microbacteraceae:**

Decreases summer into fall, increases winter into spring. All the same
genus *Rhodoglobus* within actinobacteria. Species of *Rhodoglobus*
change from summer into spring. Some other speices cultured from an
antarctic lake and able to work at 5degC.

### Rhodoglobus Plot

```{r}
# Clone dataset
taxa <- "Rhodoglobus" # taxa of interest
n <- 5 # Number of taxa to included in final graph
mt_sylph_subset <- clone(mt_sylph)

###mt_sylph_subset include the taxa below
mt_sylph_subset$tax_table <- mt_sylph_subset$tax_table |> 
  filter(g == taxa)
mt_sylph_subset$tidy_dataset()

# check
mt_sylph_subset # OK
head(mt_sylph_subset$tax_table)

# recalculate abundances (needed?)
mt_sylph_subset$cal_abund()
mt_sylph_subset$taxa_abund

# plot:

t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "s", ntaxa = n)
t1$plot_bar(facet = "strat_season") +
  ggtitle(taxa)
```

### Microbacteraceae Plot

![](output/mag_plots/f_microbacteriaceae_abund_mag.png)

## UBA 1268

#### Trends

Drops a lot going into the winter time, doesn't return coming into
spring time. QWPN01 dominates at first but then BAIKAL-G1-2R takes over.

```{r}
# Clone dataset
taxa <- "Pirellulales" # taxa of interest
n <- 5 # Number of taxa to included in final graph
mt_sylph_subset <- clone(mt_sylph)

###mt_sylph_subset include the taxa below
mt_sylph_subset$tax_table <- mt_sylph_subset$tax_table |> 
  filter(o == taxa)
mt_sylph_subset$tidy_dataset()

# check
mt_sylph_subset # OK
head(mt_sylph_subset$tax_table)

# recalculate abundances (needed?)
mt_sylph_subset$cal_abund()
mt_sylph_subset$taxa_abund

# plot:

t1 <- trans_abund$new(dataset = mt_sylph_subset, taxrank = "g", ntaxa = n)
t1$plot_bar(facet = "strat_season") +
  ggtitle(taxa)
```

Scratch that. Talked with Cody today and he said we should be looking at
the 16s data as it has a less sensitive cutoff so may be more "accurate"
than the sylph data. Updated binning script by checking each part
individually, removed the 'environment' option fom the multi binning
part, and changed the delimiter in the final contigs file to look for
'\_' instead of ':'. Once each one worked, I started the whole binning
script and we will see how long it takes.

-   Did some research into lefSE and using that in microeco. The
    resulting code is at the bottom of 16s analysis. The next thing to
    do is to look at what taxa are coming out there.
-   See if I can get some other data from Jay. - CHECK
-   Start laying out some figures (read a couple of papers)
-   Email Stormi about outside thesis committee folks - CHECK

# March 6, 2026

Loaded maestro data into R. There is bad data at the beginning of the
deployment for the first two days. Then some of the sensors crap out at
the end (pCO2). **TODO:** - Figure out how to best deal with outliers in
the variables - Average data and add to metadata - Ask Cody about how to
best average the environmental data - Reorganize scripts to output the
.csvs and load in the rest from excel files - CHECK - Use Bill's script
to average the other data -CHECK

# March 17, 2026

Worked a lot today on the maestro data and tried to clean it up. Removed
visually obvious outliers. made 24hr averages and added them to the meta
data file. Decided on a 1degC temperature difference from the maestro to
the closest thermistor to determine whether we could incorporate the
maestro data into our analysis. Made my '01_metadata_import_clean.R'
easy to just source or I can take the data products from my data folder.

**Outstanding questions:** - How to deal with outliers in oxygen data -
Is a 1 degree change close enough for us to assume the water column is
mixing? - pH and turbidity data

# March 19, 2026

**TODO:** Figure out differential abundance tests and why to use one
over the other - Continue to calculate relative abundance **Notes:**Cody
mentioned looking at the coefficient of variance to check whether things
are diurnal or not. I wonder if it would make more sense to just take
the average for everything so it is all the same? Maybe I'll go variable
by variable.

# March 24, 2026

I got the mag data into a microtable, ran a LEfSe on it but nothing came
up significant. I also looked at collinearity of the evironmental data .
The JACTMZ01, sediminibacterium, and UBA10103 genuses were all the
largest differences.

```{r}
# View sample table 

t1 <- trans_diff$new(dataset = mt_mag, method = "lefse", group = "strat_season", alpha = 0.1, taxa_level = 'g', lefse_subgroup = NULL)

t1$plot_diff_bar(threshold = 2)
# we show 30 taxa with the highest LDA (log10)
t1$plot_diff_bar(use_number = 1:30, width = 0.8)
```

```{r}
#NMDS for MAG data
tb2 <- trans_beta$new(mt_sylph, group = "strat_season", measure = "bray")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "NMDS")
tb2$cal_manova(manova_all = TRUE)
print(tb2$res_manova)

tb2$plot_ordination(plot_color = "strat_season", plot_shape = "strat_season", plot_type = c("point", "ellipse"), NMDS_stress_text_prefix = "", ellipse_level = 0.95) +
  #   geom_text(
  #   data = tb2$dataset$otu_table, # Filter data for the label layer
  #   aes(label = sample),
  #   vjust = -1.1, # Adjust vertical position
  #   hjust = 0.5,  # Adjust horizontal position
  #   color = "black"
  # ) +
  # geom_arrow_chain(colour = "black") + 
    ggtitle("Sylph NMDS") +
  # scale_color_viridis_b() +
  NULL


  #PCA?
tb2 <- trans_beta$new(mt_sylph, group = "strat_season", measure = "bray")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "PCA")
tb2$cal_manova(manova_all = TRUE)
print(tb2$res_manova)

tb2$plot_ordination(plot_color = "strat_season", plot_shape = "strat_season", plot_type = c("point", "ellipse"), loading_arrow = T, ellipse_level = 0.95) +
  #   geom_text(
  #   data = tb2$dataset$otu_table, # Filter data for the label layer
  #   aes(label = sample),
  #   vjust = -1.1, # Adjust vertical position
  #   hjust = 0.5,  # Adjust horizontal position
  #   color = "black"
  # ) +
  # geom_arrow_chain(colour = "black") + 
    ggtitle("Sylph NMDS") +
  # scale_color_viridis_b() +
  NULL

```

**TODO:** - Bug Cody about the metabolic data - Look more into how the
taxa change - Plot PCA and update the NMDS to have the arrows - Figure
out all the statistics I should be doing

# March 27, 2026

-   Working through a book to figure out the stats
    https://uw.pressbooks.pub/appliedmultivariatestatistics/chapter/relativizations/
    -   Currently trying to figure out which normalization to do on the
        abundance data. "Gower and X\^2 distances perform better than
        other measures in revealing the relationships among the samples
        along an environmental gradient even under low sequencing depth
        and the presence of noise in simulating abundance vectors The
        Jaccard dissimilarity measure was shown to perform well in
        revealing the cluster relationships when such relationships were
        prominent, but not very well for subtle clusters. The χ 2
        distance was shown to perform well in revealing environmental
        gradients, but only moderately well in revealing underlying
        clusters." -
        -   Statistical Analysis of micrbiome data (pg 84)

It's hard for me to tell from this which to use. This doesn't seem to be
quite the right answer.

-   As reccomended by the 'performance' package documentation, I called
    outliers based on MAD (median absolute deviation) instead of the
    mean in the environmental data.

\# March 30, 2026

Continuing to work on stats and figuring out the right ones. Should look
into **UniFrac** to see if that makes the most sense.

Chatting with Stormi about Joe as external committee member, need a CV
and a a letter signed by Cody that says why we want him to be a
committee member.

Compositional Samples: Samples that describe the relative composition of
organisms in a sample. This ignore the total number of organisms
counted. For example in WM01 we might have gotten 100 reads back that
mapped to A B C and D equally. However in WM02 we only got a 10 reads
back that mapped to A B C and D equally. These samples are counted as
equals. However if we were out taking net samples. A sample with only 10
individuals would be different than our sample with 100 individuals.
Because we cannot standardize the number of reads very well we think of
microbial samples as compositional -- we care about their composition
rather than absolute abundances. IE everything is already normalized to
0 to 1..

Also looked at a couple other books like Tuomas Borman, Leo Lahti,
Sudarshan Shetty, Felix M Ernst, et al. 2026. Orchestrating Microbiome
Analysis with Bioconductor \[Beta Version\]. microbiome.github.io/oma/.
on github.

Based on information from: Borman et al., (2026), and Datta and Guha
(2022 pdf), it seems like I should use a clr or rclr transformation and
then Aitchison distantances rather than Bray-Curtis. Did that to
calculate Aitchison distances and then did a PCA based in information
from...

```{r}
    tb2 <- trans_beta$new(mt_mag, group = "strat_season", measure = "aitchison")
tb2$cal_ordination(method = "PCA")
tb2$cal_manova(manova_all = TRUE)
print(tb2$res_manova)

tb2$plot_ordination(plot_color = "strat_season", plot_shape = "strat_season", plot_type = c("point", "ellipse"), ellipse_level = 0.95) +
  #   geom_text(
  #   data = tb2$dataset$otu_table, # Filter data for the label layer
  #   aes(label = sample),
  #   vjust = -1.1, # Adjust vertical position
  #   hjust = 0.5,  # Adjust horizontal position
  #   color = "black"
  # ) +
  # geom_arrow_chain(colour = "black") + 
    ggtitle("MAGs PCA") +
  # scale_color_viridis_b() +
  NULL
```

Right now have all the data sets with three dissimilarity tests: Bray, Aitchison, and robust Aitchison. All three show significance for the MANOVA but the plots look slightly different. The sylph/bray data has the nicest arrows pointing around. 
PCoA explain about 50% of the variation which is pretty cool. 

Looks like the Diamond (2019) soil paper used weighted unifraq for distance measurements. 

**TODO:**
  - Look at what variables explain most of the data
  - See if LefSE is the best option or not to look for interesting classes
  - Metabolic data from Cody
  - Import trees for unifrac stuff


# March 31, 2026

Meeting with Jay - Maestro Data
Probably just trim off the last month of data, 
DO umol/L about 12 mg/L typically, check to see what reasonble values are, compare DO to thermocline. Take a look at whether high values are above the thermocline or not. Look at saturation.

Period tends to be about 17 hours, near-initial velocity.
PCO2  looks diurnal starting in May. Convection from heating make surface water higher than 4c and sinks. 
Dan Sanborne - pCO2 paper - neural network based estimate of 
pC02 - ppm (uatm)
CHLa seeing photobleaching in the diurnal signal. 

Temperature Data
01 - Below thermocline, stable temp
02 - Below thermocline, stable temp
03 - Below thermocline, stable temp
04 - Below thermocline, stable temp
05 - Mostly below thermocline, couple big spikes after noon but noon temp would work
11 - isothermal, noon temp
12 - isothermal, noon temp
13 - isothermal, noon temp
14 - isothermal, noon temp
15 - isothermal, noon temp
16 - isothermal, noon temp
17 - isothermal, noon temp
18 - isothermal, noon temp
19 - isothermal, noon temp
20 - isothermal, noon temp
21 - isothermal, noon temp
22 - isothermal, noon temp
23 - isothermal, noon temp
24 - light thermocline, below thermocline stable temp

Based on temperature graphs it seems like I should just do the median value because there might be some variations in temperature especially in the March months when we have convective cooling and the community in those areas would be a reflection of that fluctuation in temperature. 

Spent most of the day going through the environmental stuff. I feel pretty good about it so far. 
**Next Steps:** Finish looking at pH. Check out the end of the deployment and the periodicity of it.
  - Look at values from Minor to see what is normal
  - Look at oxygen values and how they fluctuate with temperature. I might need to get rid of some of the values before averaging them so I am only measuring below the thermocline
